subject: Real Property
coverage_standard: Expanded NCBE-aligned Real Property rule bank covering ownership of real property, rights in real property, real estate contracts and conveyancing, mortgages and security devices, title and recording, adverse possession, and related land use doctrines reflected in the MBE outline.

rules.title: Fee Simple Absolute
rules.prompt_question: What is a fee simple absolute?
rules.rule_statement: A fee simple absolute is the largest estate in land, of potentially infinite duration, and is freely alienable, devisable, and descendible.
rules.application_example: O conveys 'to A and his heirs.' A takes a fee simple absolute.
rules.common_trap: Words of inheritance are no longer generally required to create a fee simple absolute.

rules.title: Fee Tail
rules.prompt_question: What is a fee tail?
rules.rule_statement: A fee tail is an estate that descends automatically to the grantee's lineal descendants and, in most jurisdictions, is either abolished or converted into a fee simple absolute.
rules.application_example: O conveys 'to A and the heirs of his body.' Historically this created a fee tail.
rules.common_trap: Most modern jurisdictions do not recognize fee tail as a distinct estate.

rules.title: Life Estate
rules.prompt_question: What is a life estate?
rules.rule_statement: A life estate is an estate measured by the life of a person and ends automatically at that person's death.
rules.application_example: O conveys 'to A for life, then to B.' A has a life estate.
rules.common_trap: A life tenant cannot devise the property beyond the measuring life.

rules.title: Life Estate Pur Autre Vie
rules.prompt_question: What is a life estate pur autre vie?
rules.rule_statement: A life estate pur autre vie is a life estate measured by the life of someone other than the holder of the estate.
rules.application_example: O conveys 'to A for the life of B.' A has a life estate pur autre vie.
rules.common_trap: The estate ends when the measuring life dies, even if the holder is still alive.

rules.title: Defeasible Fees
rules.prompt_question: What is a defeasible fee?
rules.rule_statement: A defeasible fee is a fee simple that may end upon the occurrence or nonoccurrence of a specified event.
rules.application_example: O conveys land to a school 'so long as it is used for school purposes.'
rules.common_trap: The future interest depends on the precise wording used.

rules.title: Fee Simple Determinable
rules.prompt_question: What is a fee simple determinable?
rules.rule_statement: A fee simple determinable ends automatically upon the occurrence of a stated event and is typically created by durational language such as 'so long as,' 'while,' or 'until.'
rules.application_example: O conveys 'to A so long as the land is used as a park.'
rules.common_trap: The grantor retains a possibility of reverter.

rules.title: Possibility of Reverter
rules.prompt_question: What is a possibility of reverter?
rules.rule_statement: A possibility of reverter is the future interest retained by a grantor following a fee simple determinable.
rules.application_example: If the park use stops, title automatically reverts to O.
rules.common_trap: No action is required for title to revert.

rules.title: Fee Simple Subject to Condition Subsequent
rules.prompt_question: What is a fee simple subject to condition subsequent?
rules.rule_statement: A fee simple subject to condition subsequent does not end automatically, but may be cut short at the grantor's election upon breach of a stated condition.
rules.application_example: O conveys 'to A, but if alcohol is ever sold on the land, O may reenter and retake.'
rules.common_trap: The grantor must take affirmative steps to divest the grantee.

rules.title: Right of Entry
rules.prompt_question: What is a right of entry?
rules.rule_statement: A right of entry, also called a power of termination, is the future interest retained by a grantor following a fee simple subject to condition subsequent.
rules.application_example: Upon breach, O may choose whether to retake the land.
rules.common_trap: Unlike a possibility of reverter, the estate does not terminate automatically.

rules.title: Fee Simple Subject to Executory Limitation
rules.prompt_question: What is a fee simple subject to executory limitation?
rules.rule_statement: A fee simple subject to executory limitation is a defeasible fee that, upon the stated event, automatically shifts to a third party rather than reverting to the grantor.
rules.application_example: O conveys 'to A so long as the land is used as a farm, then to B.'
rules.common_trap: The third party holds an executory interest.

rules.title: Reversion
rules.prompt_question: What is a reversion?
rules.rule_statement: A reversion is a future interest retained by a grantor who conveys less than the grantor's entire estate.
rules.application_example: O conveys 'to A for life.' O retains a reversion.
rules.common_trap: A reversion becomes possessory naturally upon expiration of the prior estate.

rules.title: Remainder
rules.prompt_question: What is a remainder?
rules.rule_statement: A remainder is a future interest created in a transferee that becomes possessory upon the natural expiration of the prior estate and does not cut it short.
rules.application_example: O conveys 'to A for life, then to B.' B has a remainder.
rules.common_trap: A remainder cannot follow a fee simple absolute or cut short a prior estate.

rules.title: Vested Remainder
rules.prompt_question: When is a remainder vested?
rules.rule_statement: A remainder is vested if it is given to an ascertained person and is not subject to a condition precedent other than the natural termination of the preceding estate.
rules.application_example: O conveys 'to A for life, then to B.' B has a vested remainder.
rules.common_trap: Words of condition can make the interest contingent instead.

rules.title: Indefeasibly Vested Remainder
rules.prompt_question: What is an indefeasibly vested remainder?
rules.rule_statement: An indefeasibly vested remainder is certain to become possessory in the future and cannot be divested.
rules.application_example: O conveys 'to A for life, then to B.' B has an indefeasibly vested remainder.
rules.common_trap: No later event can cut off the taker.

rules.title: Vested Remainder Subject to Complete Divestment
rules.prompt_question: What is a vested remainder subject to complete divestment?
rules.rule_statement: A vested remainder subject to complete divestment is vested in an ascertained person but may be entirely divested by a subsequent condition.
rules.application_example: O conveys 'to A for life, then to B, but if B does not survive A, to C.'
rules.common_trap: B's interest is vested because there is no condition precedent to taking.

rules.title: Vested Remainder Subject to Open
rules.prompt_question: What is a vested remainder subject to open?
rules.rule_statement: A vested remainder subject to open is a vested remainder held by at least one ascertained member of a class that may increase in size.
rules.application_example: O conveys 'to A for life, then to A's children,' where A has one living child.
rules.common_trap: The class closes under the rule of convenience when any member is entitled to possession.

rules.title: Contingent Remainder
rules.prompt_question: When is a remainder contingent?
rules.rule_statement: A remainder is contingent if it is either given to an unascertained person or subject to a condition precedent.
rules.application_example: O conveys 'to A for life, then to B if B graduates law school.'
rules.common_trap: The condition must occur before the remainder can become possessory.

rules.title: Executory Interest
rules.prompt_question: What is an executory interest?
rules.rule_statement: An executory interest is a future interest in a transferee that divests or cuts short another interest or begins after a gap in possession.
rules.application_example: O conveys 'to A, but if alcohol is sold on the land, then to B.'
rules.common_trap: Executory interests are subject to the Rule Against Perpetuities.

rules.title: Shifting and Springing Executory Interests
rules.prompt_question: What is the difference between shifting and springing executory interests?
rules.rule_statement: A shifting executory interest divests another transferee, while a springing executory interest divests the grantor or follows a gap in possession.
rules.application_example: An interest in B that divests A is shifting; one that divests O is springing.
rules.common_trap: Both are executory interests and both may violate the RAP.

rules.title: Rule in Shelley's Case
rules.prompt_question: What is the Rule in Shelley's Case?
rules.rule_statement: At common law, the Rule in Shelley's Case converted certain future interests given to the grantee's heirs into a remainder in the grantee, but the rule is abolished in most jurisdictions.
rules.application_example: Old conveyances 'to A for life, then to A's heirs' could merge under the rule.
rules.common_trap: Usually tested only as historical background unless facts say the rule applies.

rules.title: Doctrine of Worthier Title
rules.prompt_question: What is the Doctrine of Worthier Title?
rules.rule_statement: The Doctrine of Worthier Title treated a remainder in the grantor's heirs as a reversion in the grantor, but most jurisdictions have abolished or limited the doctrine.
rules.application_example: O conveys 'to A for life, then to O's heirs.' Historically O retained a reversion.
rules.common_trap: Often not applied today.

rules.title: Destructibility of Contingent Remainders
rules.prompt_question: What was the destructibility doctrine for contingent remainders?
rules.rule_statement: At common law, a contingent remainder had to vest at or before termination of the preceding freehold estate or it was destroyed, but most jurisdictions have abolished this rule.
rules.application_example: A contingent remainder failing to vest on time could be destroyed at common law.
rules.common_trap: Modern courts usually preserve the interest as an executory interest or otherwise save it.

rules.title: Waste
rules.prompt_question: What is waste?
rules.rule_statement: Waste is conduct by a holder of a possessory estate, such as a life tenant, that unreasonably harms the interests of future interest holders.
rules.application_example: A life tenant tears down a valuable structure without justification.
rules.common_trap: Different types of waste have different rules and remedies.

rules.title: Voluntary Waste
rules.prompt_question: What is voluntary waste?
rules.rule_statement: Voluntary waste is affirmative conduct that reduces the property's value, such as exploitation of natural resources beyond permitted exceptions.
rules.application_example: A life tenant opens a new quarry on the land.
rules.common_trap: Existing mines may usually continue under the open-mines doctrine.

rules.title: Permissive Waste
rules.prompt_question: What is permissive waste?
rules.rule_statement: Permissive waste is neglect by the tenant in possession, such as failure to maintain the property, pay taxes, or pay interest on mortgages to the extent required.
rules.application_example: A life tenant allows the roof to rot and property taxes to go unpaid.
rules.common_trap: Life tenants need not make extraordinary improvements.

rules.title: Ameliorative Waste
rules.prompt_question: What is ameliorative waste?
rules.rule_statement: Ameliorative waste is a substantial change to property that increases its value but alters its character, and modern courts may allow it in changed-neighborhood circumstances.
rules.application_example: A life tenant converts an old mansion into apartments in a fully commercial neighborhood.
rules.common_trap: Traditional common law was hostile to major changes even if profitable.

rules.title: Tenancy in Common
rules.prompt_question: What is a tenancy in common?
rules.rule_statement: A tenancy in common is a concurrent estate in which each cotenant owns an undivided fractional interest that is freely transferable, devisable, and descendible, and there is no right of survivorship.
rules.application_example: O conveys 'to A and B.' In most jurisdictions, A and B take as tenants in common.
rules.common_trap: Unequal shares are allowed in a tenancy in common.

rules.title: Joint Tenancy
rules.prompt_question: What is a joint tenancy?
rules.rule_statement: A joint tenancy is a concurrent estate with right of survivorship that traditionally requires the four unities of time, title, interest, and possession.
rules.application_example: O conveys 'to A and B as joint tenants with right of survivorship.'
rules.common_trap: Clear survivorship language is usually required.

rules.title: Tenancy by the Entirety
rules.prompt_question: What is tenancy by the entirety?
rules.rule_statement: Tenancy by the entirety is a marital concurrent estate with right of survivorship in which neither spouse acting alone may sever or convey the estate in most jurisdictions.
rules.application_example: Spouses taking title together in some jurisdictions may take by the entirety by default.
rules.common_trap: Available only to married couples and may protect against individual creditors depending on jurisdiction.

rules.title: Severance of Joint Tenancy
rules.prompt_question: How is a joint tenancy severed?
rules.rule_statement: A joint tenancy is severed when one joint tenant destroys one of the four unities, such as by conveying that tenant's interest to a third party.
rules.application_example: A sells A's joint tenancy share to C; C and B become tenants in common as to that share.
rules.common_trap: At common law, a mortgage could sever under title theory but not under lien theory.

rules.title: Possession Rights of Cotenants
rules.prompt_question: What possession rights do cotenants have?
rules.rule_statement: Each cotenant has an equal right to possess and enjoy the whole property, regardless of the size of that cotenant's fractional share, subject to equal rights of the others.
rules.application_example: A 10 percent cotenant may still occupy the whole property so long as others are not excluded.
rules.common_trap: Exclusive occupancy alone does not usually require rent payment to absent cotenants absent ouster or agreement.

rules.title: Ouster
rules.prompt_question: What is ouster among cotenants?
rules.rule_statement: Ouster occurs when one cotenant wrongfully excludes another cotenant from possession, giving rise to a possible claim for rent and often starting adverse possession if other requirements are met.
rules.application_example: A changes locks and tells B that B may never enter again.
rules.common_trap: Mere exclusive possession without exclusion is not necessarily ouster.

rules.title: Rent and Expenses Among Cotenants
rules.prompt_question: How are rent and expenses allocated among cotenants?
rules.rule_statement: A cotenant in sole possession generally owes no rent to other cotenants absent ouster or agreement, but cotenants are entitled to contribution for necessary carrying costs such as taxes and mortgage interest, and may receive credits for repairs or improvements in partition.
rules.application_example: A pays all property taxes and may seek contribution from B.
rules.common_trap: Improvement costs are usually handled at partition, not immediate contribution.

rules.title: Partition
rules.prompt_question: What is partition?
rules.rule_statement: Any cotenant has a right to partition, either in kind by physical division or by sale and division of proceeds when partition in kind is impracticable or unfair.
rules.application_example: B may seek judicial partition of farmland jointly owned with A.
rules.common_trap: Modern courts often prefer partition in kind unless a sale is clearly more equitable.

rules.title: Tenancy for Years
rules.prompt_question: What is a tenancy for years?
rules.rule_statement: A tenancy for years is a leasehold for a fixed period that ends automatically at the stated date without notice.
rules.application_example: A one-year apartment lease is a tenancy for years.
rules.common_trap: No notice is needed to terminate at the end of the term.

rules.title: Periodic Tenancy
rules.prompt_question: What is a periodic tenancy?
rules.rule_statement: A periodic tenancy continues for successive periods until properly terminated by notice from either the landlord or the tenant.
rules.application_example: A month-to-month lease is a periodic tenancy.
rules.common_trap: Notice timing rules often depend on the length of the period or statute.

rules.title: Tenancy at Will
rules.prompt_question: What is a tenancy at will?
rules.rule_statement: A tenancy at will is a leasehold of no fixed duration that continues only so long as both landlord and tenant desire.
rules.application_example: L lets T stay 'for as long as both of us agree.'
rules.common_trap: Modern statutes often require some notice to terminate.

rules.title: Tenancy at Sufferance
rules.prompt_question: What is a tenancy at sufferance?
rules.rule_statement: A tenancy at sufferance arises when a tenant wrongfully remains in possession after the lease term ends.
rules.application_example: T stays in the apartment after expiration of the one-year lease.
rules.common_trap: Landlord may evict or bind the tenant to a new tenancy under the governing rules.

rules.title: Delivery of Possession
rules.prompt_question: What possession must the landlord deliver at the start of the lease?
rules.rule_statement: Under the majority English rule, the landlord must deliver actual possession at the start of the lease; under the minority American rule, the landlord need only deliver legal possession.
rules.application_example: If a holdover squatter remains on move-in day, landlord may be in breach under the English rule.
rules.common_trap: Know the majority English rule.

rules.title: Tenant's Duty to Pay Rent
rules.prompt_question: What is the tenant's rent obligation?
rules.rule_statement: The tenant must pay rent as promised unless the duty is excused by a recognized landlord breach or other defense.
rules.application_example: T must continue rent payments under the lease unless constructive eviction or warranty breach applies.
rules.common_trap: Traditional common law treated covenants as independent, but modern rules often soften this harshness in residential leases.

rules.title: Assignment Versus Sublease
rules.prompt_question: What is the difference between an assignment and a sublease?
rules.rule_statement: An assignment transfers the tenant's entire remaining leasehold interest, while a sublease transfers less than the tenant's entire remaining interest.
rules.application_example: If T transfers the last six months of a one-year lease, that is an assignment; if T keeps a reversion of one day, it is a sublease.
rules.common_trap: Liability turns on privity of estate and privity of contract.

rules.title: Privity After Assignment
rules.prompt_question: What liability follows an assignment?
rules.rule_statement: An assignee in possession is in privity of estate with the landlord and is liable on lease covenants that run with the land, while the original tenant generally remains liable on the lease by privity of contract unless released.
rules.application_example: Landlord may sue assignee for accruing rent and original tenant for contract liability.
rules.common_trap: An assignee who reassigns may end future estate-based liability.

rules.title: Liability After Sublease
rules.prompt_question: What liability follows a sublease?
rules.rule_statement: A sublessee is generally not in privity of estate or privity of contract with the landlord and is usually liable only to the original tenant absent an agreement with the landlord.
rules.application_example: If T subleases to S, landlord ordinarily sues T rather than S for rent.
rules.common_trap: The landlord may still have tort or nuisance claims directly against the sublessee.

rules.title: Landlord Consent to Transfer
rules.prompt_question: May a landlord refuse consent to assignment or sublease?
rules.rule_statement: If the lease requires landlord consent before transfer, the landlord may withhold consent, but in many jurisdictions residential and commercial leases imply a reasonableness requirement unless the lease gives absolute discretion.
rules.application_example: Landlord may not arbitrarily refuse a financially sound replacement tenant in jurisdictions applying reasonableness.
rules.common_trap: Check the lease language and jurisdiction rule.

rules.title: Surrender
rules.prompt_question: What is surrender of a lease?
rules.rule_statement: Surrender is an agreement between landlord and tenant that the tenant will give up the leasehold before the term ends, terminating future obligations.
rules.application_example: T returns keys and landlord accepts them as ending the lease.
rules.common_trap: Merely vacating is not necessarily surrender unless accepted by landlord.

rules.title: Constructive Eviction
rules.prompt_question: What is constructive eviction?
rules.rule_statement: Constructive eviction occurs when the landlord's wrongful conduct substantially interferes with the tenant's use and enjoyment, the tenant gives notice and time to cure if appropriate, and the tenant vacates within a reasonable time.
rules.application_example: Landlord repeatedly fails to fix noxious sewage leaks, forcing T to leave.
rules.common_trap: Tenant must actually leave to claim constructive eviction.

rules.title: Partial Actual Eviction
rules.prompt_question: What is the effect of partial actual eviction?
rules.rule_statement: If the landlord physically excludes the tenant from part of the premises, the tenant may generally remain and withhold all rent at common law, though some modern jurisdictions apportion rent.
rules.application_example: Landlord blocks tenant from using one room included in the lease.
rules.common_trap: This differs from constructive eviction, which requires the tenant to vacate.

rules.title: Implied Warranty of Habitability
rules.prompt_question: What is the implied warranty of habitability?
rules.rule_statement: In residential leases, the landlord impliedly warrants that the premises are fit for human habitation and will remain so, and the tenant may seek rent abatement, repair-and-deduct, damages, or termination for substantial breaches.
rules.application_example: No heat in winter may breach the warranty.
rules.common_trap: This warranty generally cannot be waived in residential leases.

rules.title: Covenant of Quiet Enjoyment
rules.prompt_question: What is the covenant of quiet enjoyment?
rules.rule_statement: The landlord impliedly promises that the tenant's possession will not be disturbed by the landlord or someone with superior title.
rules.application_example: Landlord repeatedly enters and disrupts possession, breaching quiet enjoyment.
rules.common_trap: Quiet enjoyment supports constructive eviction in serious cases.

rules.title: Retaliatory Eviction
rules.prompt_question: What is retaliatory eviction?
rules.rule_statement: A landlord may not evict or otherwise retaliate against a residential tenant for reporting housing-code violations or exercising legal rights.
rules.application_example: Landlord raises rent after tenant complains to code enforcement.
rules.common_trap: This defense is often statutory.

rules.title: Fair Housing
rules.prompt_question: What conduct is prohibited by fair housing law?
rules.rule_statement: Federal fair housing law generally prohibits discrimination in the sale or rental of housing on the basis of race, color, religion, sex, familial status, national origin, and disability, subject to limited exceptions.
rules.application_example: A landlord may not refuse to rent to a family because they have children.
rules.common_trap: There are narrow owner-occupied and other statutory exceptions.

rules.title: Security Deposits
rules.prompt_question: What rules commonly govern security deposits?
rules.rule_statement: Modern statutes commonly regulate the amount, handling, and return of security deposits and may require itemized statements of deductions.
rules.application_example: Landlord must often return the deposit within a set statutory period after move-out.
rules.common_trap: This area is highly statutory.

rules.title: Mitigation of Damages
rules.prompt_question: Must a landlord mitigate damages when a tenant abandons?
rules.rule_statement: In most modern jurisdictions, the landlord must make reasonable efforts to mitigate damages when a tenant abandons the premises before the lease term ends.
rules.application_example: Landlord must attempt to re-let the apartment after T moves out.
rules.common_trap: Traditional common law imposed no duty to mitigate, but the modern trend is the opposite.

rules.title: Easement
rules.prompt_question: What is an easement?
rules.rule_statement: An easement is a nonpossessory right to use another's land for a particular purpose.
rules.application_example: A right-of-way over a driveway is an easement.
rules.common_trap: An easement does not give a right to possess the land.

rules.title: Appurtenant and In Gross Easements
rules.prompt_question: What is the difference between an appurtenant easement and an easement in gross?
rules.rule_statement: An easement appurtenant benefits a parcel of land and runs with that land, while an easement in gross benefits a person or entity independent of ownership of a parcel.
rules.application_example: A utility company's line easement is often in gross.
rules.common_trap: Commercial easements in gross are usually transferable; personal ones often are not.

rules.title: Express Easement
rules.prompt_question: How is an express easement created?
rules.rule_statement: An express easement is created by a writing satisfying the Statute of Frauds or by deed reservation or grant.
rules.application_example: O deeds Blackacre to A and grants a driveway easement over Whiteacre.
rules.common_trap: Ambiguous language may create litigation over scope.

rules.title: Easement by Implication
rules.prompt_question: When does an easement by implication arise?
rules.rule_statement: An easement by implication may arise when there was prior common ownership, prior use before severance, the use was apparent and continuous, and the easement is reasonably necessary to enjoyment of the dominant parcel.
rules.application_example: After subdivision, the visible driveway historically used to reach the back parcel may continue by implication.
rules.common_trap: The level of necessity is usually less than for necessity easements.

rules.title: Easement by Necessity
rules.prompt_question: When does an easement by necessity arise?
rules.rule_statement: An easement by necessity arises when a parcel is severed from common ownership and one part is left landlocked, making access strictly necessary.
rules.application_example: O sells the front parcel and leaves the back parcel without road access.
rules.common_trap: The necessity must exist at the time of severance.

rules.title: Prescriptive Easement
rules.prompt_question: What is required for a prescriptive easement?
rules.rule_statement: A prescriptive easement arises from use that is open and notorious, adverse, continuous, and for the statutory period.
rules.application_example: Neighbors openly use a path across land without permission for decades.
rules.common_trap: Exclusive use is not required for a prescriptive easement.

rules.title: Scope of Easement
rules.prompt_question: How is the scope of an easement determined?
rules.rule_statement: The scope of an easement is determined by the grant or the circumstances of creation and may not be unreasonably expanded beyond the contemplated use.
rules.application_example: A driveway easement for residential access may not automatically permit heavy commercial trucking.
rules.common_trap: Reasonable changes in normal use may still be allowed.

rules.title: Termination of Easement
rules.prompt_question: How can an easement be terminated?
rules.rule_statement: An easement may be terminated by release, merger, abandonment, estoppel, prescription, end of necessity, condemnation, or destruction of the servient estate.
rules.application_example: If the dominant and servient estates come into common ownership, the easement terminates by merger.
rules.common_trap: Mere nonuse alone is generally insufficient to show abandonment.

rules.title: Profit
rules.prompt_question: What is a profit?
rules.rule_statement: A profit is a nonpossessory interest allowing the holder to enter another's land and remove natural resources such as timber, minerals, or game.
rules.application_example: A timber company may hold a profit to harvest trees.
rules.common_trap: Rules governing easements often apply by analogy to profits.

rules.title: License
rules.prompt_question: What is a license?
rules.rule_statement: A license is a revocable privilege to enter another's land and does not create an interest in land.
rules.application_example: A movie ticket grants a license to enter the theater.
rules.common_trap: A license may become temporarily irrevocable under estoppel in some circumstances.

rules.title: Real Covenant Burden
rules.prompt_question: When does the burden of a real covenant run with the land?
rules.rule_statement: For the burden of a real covenant to run at law, there must be a writing, intent, the covenant must touch and concern the land, vertical privity, and usually horizontal privity, plus notice for a successor.
rules.application_example: A promise to maintain a common wall may bind a successor if the elements are met.
rules.common_trap: Horizontal privity is the difficult element and is not required for equitable servitudes.

rules.title: Real Covenant Benefit
rules.prompt_question: When does the benefit of a real covenant run with the land?
rules.rule_statement: The benefit of a real covenant runs if there is a writing, intent, the covenant touches and concerns the land, and vertical privity in most jurisdictions.
rules.application_example: Successor to the benefited parcel may enforce a covenant to contribute to road maintenance.
rules.common_trap: Horizontal privity is generally not required for the benefit to run.

rules.title: Equitable Servitude
rules.prompt_question: What is required to enforce an equitable servitude?
rules.rule_statement: An equitable servitude is enforceable in equity if there is a writing or common scheme, intent that the promise run, the promise touches and concerns the land, and the burdened party has notice.
rules.application_example: A homeowners association may enforce subdivision restrictions against a purchaser with notice.
rules.common_trap: No privity is required for equitable servitudes.

rules.title: Reciprocal Negative Servitude
rules.prompt_question: What is a reciprocal negative servitude?
rules.rule_statement: Under a common development scheme, reciprocal negative servitudes may arise so that lot owners can enforce uniform restrictions against other lots if purchasers had notice of the scheme.
rules.application_example: A subdivision map and consistent covenants may create reciprocal servitudes.
rules.common_trap: Notice may be actual, record, or inquiry notice.

rules.title: Termination of Covenants and Servitudes
rules.prompt_question: How can restrictive covenants and servitudes be terminated?
rules.rule_statement: Restrictive covenants and servitudes may terminate by release, merger, acquiescence, abandonment, estoppel, changed conditions, condemnation, or prescription.
rules.application_example: If a neighborhood becomes fully industrial, residential-only restrictions may become unenforceable.
rules.common_trap: Changed conditions must substantially defeat the purpose of the restriction.

rules.title: Common Interest Communities
rules.prompt_question: What rules commonly apply in common interest communities?
rules.rule_statement: Recorded declarations, bylaws, and association rules in common interest communities may create enforceable servitudes and obligations, subject to reasonableness and statutory protections.
rules.application_example: An HOA may collect assessments and enforce architectural standards under the recorded declaration.
rules.common_trap: Association actions may be limited by statutes and fiduciary duties.

rules.title: Statute of Frauds
rules.prompt_question: What does the Statute of Frauds require for land sale contracts?
rules.rule_statement: A contract for the sale of an interest in land must generally be in writing, signed by the party to be charged, and contain the essential terms.
rules.application_example: A signed memo identifying the parties, property, and price may satisfy the statute.
rules.common_trap: Oral land contracts are generally unenforceable unless an exception applies.

rules.title: Part Performance
rules.prompt_question: When does part performance remove an oral land contract from the Statute of Frauds?
rules.rule_statement: Part performance may permit enforcement of an oral land contract when the buyer's acts unmistakably indicate the existence of the contract, commonly through possession plus payment, improvements, or both, depending on jurisdiction.
rules.application_example: Buyer takes possession, pays part of the price, and builds a fence.
rules.common_trap: Mere payment alone is usually insufficient.

rules.title: Marketable Title
rules.prompt_question: What is marketable title?
rules.rule_statement: At closing, the seller must provide marketable title, meaning title reasonably free from doubt and not exposing the buyer to probable litigation.
rules.application_example: Title subject to an undisclosed easement may be unmarketable if it materially affects value or use.
rules.common_trap: Visible beneficial easements may not render title unmarketable.

rules.title: Defects Making Title Unmarketable
rules.prompt_question: What commonly makes title unmarketable?
rules.rule_statement: Title is commonly unmarketable if subject to adverse possession claims, undisclosed encumbrances, zoning violations, or other defects likely to trigger litigation.
rules.application_example: A house built in violation of setback laws may render title unmarketable.
rules.common_trap: Mere zoning restrictions themselves, if complied with, do not necessarily make title unmarketable.

rules.title: Time for Marketable Title
rules.prompt_question: When must the seller provide marketable title?
rules.rule_statement: Absent contrary agreement, the seller need only provide marketable title at closing, not at the time the contract is signed.
rules.application_example: A title defect discovered before closing can often be cured by the closing date.
rules.common_trap: Buyers sometimes sue too early without waiting for closing.

rules.title: Equitable Conversion
rules.prompt_question: What is equitable conversion?
rules.rule_statement: Under equitable conversion, once a specifically enforceable land sale contract is formed, the buyer is viewed as the equitable owner and the seller holds legal title in trust pending closing.
rules.application_example: After contract formation, the property may pass to the buyer's heirs while the purchase money passes to the seller's heirs.
rules.common_trap: This doctrine often affects risk of loss before closing.

rules.title: Risk of Loss
rules.prompt_question: Who bears the risk of loss between contract and closing?
rules.rule_statement: Under the traditional equitable conversion rule, the buyer bears the risk of casualty loss after contract formation unless the contract provides otherwise, though many modern statutes place the risk on the seller until possession or title passes.
keywords: seller until possession or title passes
rules.application_example: A house burns down after signing but before closing.
rules.common_trap: Check for a Uniform Vendor and Purchaser Risk Act or contract provision.

rules.title: Implied Warranty of Quality
rules.prompt_question: What implied warranty may apply in sales of new homes?
rules.rule_statement: A builder-vendor of a new home may impliedly warrant that the home is fit and constructed in a workmanlike manner.
rules.application_example: Buyer may sue builder for latent structural defects discovered after closing.
rules.common_trap: This warranty usually does not apply to ordinary sellers of used homes.

rules.title: Delivery of Deed
rules.prompt_question: What is required for delivery of a deed?
rules.rule_statement: A deed is effective only upon delivery, meaning the grantor manifests present intent that the deed operate to transfer an interest.
rules.application_example: Handing over a signed deed intending immediate transfer completes delivery.
rules.common_trap: Physical transfer alone is not enough without present intent.

rules.title: Acceptance of Deed
rules.prompt_question: What is required for acceptance of a deed?
rules.rule_statement: A deed must be accepted by the grantee, though acceptance is generally presumed if the conveyance is beneficial.
rules.application_example: A gift of land to B is presumed accepted if it benefits B.
rules.common_trap: A grantee may disclaim the transfer.

rules.title: Escrow Delivery
rules.prompt_question: What happens when a deed is delivered to escrow?
rules.rule_statement: If a grantor delivers a deed to an escrow agent with instructions to deliver upon satisfaction of conditions, title passes when the conditions are met and escrow is properly completed.
rules.application_example: Seller places deed in escrow pending buyer's payment of the balance.
rules.common_trap: If the grantor retains power to reclaim the deed, delivery may be ineffective.

rules.title: Quitclaim Deed
rules.prompt_question: What does a quitclaim deed convey?
rules.rule_statement: A quitclaim deed conveys whatever interest the grantor has, if any, without any covenants of title.
rules.application_example: Grantor conveys any interest in Blackacre without warranties.
rules.common_trap: A quitclaim grantee can still be a bona fide purchaser if the other elements are met.

rules.title: General Warranty Deed
rules.prompt_question: What covenants are contained in a general warranty deed?
rules.rule_statement: A general warranty deed includes the present covenants of seisin, right to convey, and against encumbrances, and the future covenants of warranty, quiet enjoyment, and further assurances.
rules.application_example: Buyer later discovers an undisclosed mortgage and may sue under the covenant against encumbrances.
rules.common_trap: Present covenants are breached, if at all, at delivery; future covenants are breached upon disturbance.

rules.title: Special Warranty Deed
rules.prompt_question: What does a special warranty deed warrant?
rules.rule_statement: A special warranty deed warrants against title defects arising only through the grantor, not defects existing before the grantor held title.
rules.application_example: Grantor promises no encumbrances were created during grantor's ownership.
rules.common_trap: It does not protect against earlier defects.

rules.title: Present Covenants
rules.prompt_question: What are present covenants in a deed?
rules.rule_statement: The present covenants are seisin, right to convey, and against encumbrances, and they are breached, if at all, at the time of conveyance.
rules.application_example: If grantor does not own the estate purportedly conveyed, the covenant of seisin is breached at delivery.
rules.common_trap: These covenants do not run with the land in the same way future covenants do.

rules.title: Future Covenants
rules.prompt_question: What are future covenants in a deed?
rules.rule_statement: The future covenants are warranty, quiet enjoyment, and further assurances, and they are breached only when the grantee is later disturbed in possession or title.
rules.application_example: A later successful eviction by someone with superior title breaches warranty and quiet enjoyment.
rules.common_trap: Future covenants run with the land to successors.

rules.title: Merger by Deed
rules.prompt_question: What is the merger doctrine in land sales?
rules.rule_statement: Upon delivery and acceptance of the deed, the land sale contract generally merges into the deed, and the buyer's rights are then determined by the deed's covenants rather than the contract, absent fraud or collateral promises.
rules.application_example: After closing, buyer usually cannot sue on earlier title promises not preserved in the deed.
rules.common_trap: Fraud and independent collateral promises may survive.

rules.title: Mortgage
rules.prompt_question: What is a mortgage?
rules.rule_statement: A mortgage is a security interest in real property that secures repayment of a debt or other obligation.
rules.application_example: Borrower gives lender a mortgage on the home to secure a loan.
rules.common_trap: The note is the personal obligation; the mortgage is the security.

rules.title: Title Theory and Lien Theory
rules.prompt_question: What is the difference between title theory and lien theory?
rules.rule_statement: In title theory states, the mortgage passes legal title to the mortgagee until satisfaction of the debt, while in lien theory states the mortgage creates only a lien and the mortgagor retains title.
rules.application_example: Whether a mortgage severs a joint tenancy may depend on title versus lien theory.
rules.common_trap: Most modern states follow lien theory.

rules.title: Equity of Redemption
rules.prompt_question: What is the equity of redemption?
rules.rule_statement: Before foreclosure, the mortgagor has the equitable right to redeem the property by paying the debt, interest, and costs, and this right cannot be clogged at the time the mortgage is created.
rules.application_example: Borrower may pay off the loan the day before foreclosure sale and redeem.
rules.common_trap: Clauses waiving redemption at the outset are generally invalid.

rules.title: Installment Land Contract
rules.prompt_question: How is an installment land contract generally treated?
rules.rule_statement: An installment land contract allows the buyer to pay the purchase price over time while the seller retains title as security until full payment, and courts often protect the buyer similarly to a mortgagor.
rules.application_example: Buyer occupies the land and pays monthly installments for years before final deed delivery.
rules.common_trap: Modern courts may require foreclosure-like protections before forfeiture.

rules.title: Transfer of the Note
rules.prompt_question: What happens when the note is transferred?
rules.rule_statement: The mortgage generally follows the note, so transfer of the debt ordinarily transfers the security interest as well.
rules.application_example: Bank endorses the promissory note to another lender, and the mortgage security follows.
rules.common_trap: Attempting to transfer the mortgage alone without the note is usually ineffective or limited.

rules.title: Assumption and Subject To
rules.prompt_question: What is the difference between taking property subject to a mortgage and assuming the mortgage?
rules.rule_statement: A buyer taking subject to a mortgage is not personally liable on the note, while a buyer who assumes the mortgage becomes personally liable to the lender.
rules.application_example: A buyer who expressly agrees to pay the mortgage has assumed it.
rules.common_trap: In either case, the land itself remains subject to foreclosure.

rules.title: Priority of Mortgages
rules.prompt_question: How is mortgage priority generally determined?
rules.rule_statement: Absent an applicable recording statute or exception, mortgage priority is generally determined by the order in which the interests are created or recorded.
rules.application_example: First-recorded mortgage usually has priority over later mortgages.
rules.common_trap: Purchase-money mortgages and subordination agreements can alter the result.

rules.title: Purchase Money Mortgage
rules.prompt_question: What priority does a purchase money mortgage have?
rules.rule_statement: A purchase money mortgage given to enable the buyer to acquire the property generally has priority over earlier liens arising through the buyer, at least as to the land purchased.
rules.application_example: Bank lends funds used to buy Blackacre and takes a contemporaneous mortgage on it.
rules.common_trap: This special priority usually applies only to the specific property purchased.

rules.title: Future Advance Mortgage
rules.prompt_question: How are future advance mortgages prioritized?
rules.rule_statement: A mortgage securing future advances may have priority from the time of initial recording, though optional advances made with notice of an intervening lien may lose priority in some jurisdictions.
rules.application_example: A construction lender records before making all draws.
rules.common_trap: Mandatory advances are often treated more favorably than optional advances.

rules.title: Foreclosure
rules.prompt_question: What is foreclosure?
rules.rule_statement: Foreclosure is the process by which the mortgagee cuts off the mortgagor's equity of redemption and sells the property to satisfy the debt.
rules.application_example: Lender forecloses after borrower defaults on payments.
rules.common_trap: Junior interests are handled according to whether they are joined in the foreclosure action.

rules.title: Effect of Foreclosure on Interests
rules.prompt_question: What happens to senior and junior interests at foreclosure?
rules.rule_statement: Foreclosure extinguishes interests junior to the foreclosing mortgage if those holders are properly joined, but it does not eliminate senior interests.
rules.application_example: A purchaser at foreclosure takes subject to any senior mortgage still on the land.
rules.common_trap: Failure to join a junior lienholder leaves that lien unaffected.

rules.title: Deficiency Judgment
rules.prompt_question: What is a deficiency judgment?
rules.rule_statement: If foreclosure sale proceeds are insufficient to satisfy the debt, the lender may generally seek a deficiency judgment against a personally liable borrower, subject to statutory limits.
rules.application_example: Home sells for less than the unpaid balance and lender sues borrower for the difference.
rules.common_trap: No deficiency against a buyer who took subject to but did not assume the mortgage.

rules.title: Distribution of Foreclosure Surplus
rules.prompt_question: How is a foreclosure surplus distributed?
rules.rule_statement: Any foreclosure surplus is distributed first to junior lienholders in order of priority and then to the mortgagor.
rules.application_example: After paying the foreclosing lender, remaining funds go down the priority ladder.
rules.common_trap: Senior liens are not paid from the surplus because they remain on the property unless separately satisfied.

rules.title: Acceleration Clauses
rules.prompt_question: What does an acceleration clause do in a mortgage note?
rules.rule_statement: An acceleration clause permits the lender, upon default, to declare the entire balance immediately due.
rules.application_example: Missing one payment may let the lender call the full loan due if the contract permits.
rules.common_trap: Notice and cure requirements may be imposed by contract or statute.

rules.title: Purpose of Recording Acts
rules.prompt_question: What is the purpose of recording acts?
rules.rule_statement: Recording acts protect bona fide purchasers and creditors by establishing priority rules among competing claimants to the same real property interest.
rules.application_example: A later good-faith buyer may defeat an earlier unrecorded deed under the applicable recording statute.
rules.common_trap: The result depends on whether the jurisdiction is race, notice, or race-notice.

rules.title: Bona Fide Purchaser
rules.prompt_question: Who is a bona fide purchaser?
rules.rule_statement: A bona fide purchaser is one who acquires an interest in property for value and without notice of a prior unrecorded interest.
rules.application_example: A purchaser paying fair price without knowledge of an earlier deed may qualify as a BFP.
rules.common_trap: Donees, heirs, and devisees are not purchasers for value.

rules.title: Types of Notice
rules.prompt_question: What types of notice can defeat bona fide purchaser status?
rules.rule_statement: Notice may be actual notice, inquiry notice arising from facts that would prompt a reasonable investigation, or record notice arising from properly recorded instruments in the chain of title.
rules.application_example: Visible possession by another can impose inquiry notice on a buyer.
rules.common_trap: A recorded instrument outside the chain of title usually does not provide record notice.

rules.title: Race Statute
rules.prompt_question: How does a race statute operate?
rules.rule_statement: In a race jurisdiction, the first grantee to record prevails, regardless of notice.
rules.application_example: If B records before A, B wins even if B knew about A.
rules.common_trap: Pure race statutes are uncommon.

rules.title: Notice Statute
rules.prompt_question: How does a notice statute operate?
rules.rule_statement: In a notice jurisdiction, a subsequent bona fide purchaser prevails over a prior unrecorded interest, even if the subsequent purchaser records later.
rules.application_example: B buys without notice of A's unrecorded deed and prevails over A.
rules.common_trap: Recording by B is not required to win under a pure notice statute, though it is prudent.

rules.title: Race-Notice Statute
rules.prompt_question: How does a race-notice statute operate?
rules.rule_statement: In a race-notice jurisdiction, a subsequent bona fide purchaser prevails only if the purchaser takes without notice and records first.
rules.application_example: B must both qualify as a BFP and win the race to the recorder's office.
rules.common_trap: Failure to record first defeats B even without notice.

rules.title: Chain of Title
rules.prompt_question: What is the chain of title?
rules.rule_statement: The chain of title is the sequence of recorded instruments that can be located through a reasonable title search under the grantor-grantee indexes or other recording system.
rules.application_example: A properly indexed deed from the record owner to the grantee is within the chain of title.
rules.common_trap: Wild deeds outside the chain of title generally do not give record notice.

rules.title: Shelter Rule
rules.prompt_question: What is the shelter rule?
rules.rule_statement: A transferee from a bona fide purchaser takes shelter in the transferor's status and receives the same priority, even if the transferee had notice.
keywords: transferee from a bona fide purchaser
rules.application_example: B is a BFP and later transfers to C, who knew of A's prior claim; C may still prevail through B.
rules.common_trap: The rule cannot be used by a party who was originally involved in fraud to wash the title.

rules.title: Wild Deed
rules.prompt_question: What is a wild deed?
rules.rule_statement: A wild deed is a recorded deed not connected to the chain of title, and it generally does not give record notice to later purchasers.
rules.application_example: A deed from X to Y recorded before X appears in the chain of title is wild.
rules.common_trap: A title searcher is not expected to find it.

rules.title: Estoppel by Deed
rules.prompt_question: What is estoppel by deed?
rules.rule_statement: If a grantor purports to convey property the grantor does not own and later acquires title, the title automatically passes to the grantee under estoppel by deed in many jurisdictions.
rules.application_example: O falsely deeds Blackacre to A before acquiring it, then later acquires it; title passes to A.
rules.common_trap: Recording acts may still affect priority against third parties.

rules.title: Title Insurance
rules.prompt_question: What is title insurance?
rules.rule_statement: Title insurance indemnifies the insured against covered title defects existing at the time the policy is issued, subject to exclusions and exceptions.
rules.application_example: A hidden forged deed in the chain may trigger title-insurance coverage.
rules.common_trap: Title insurance is not the same as the seller's deed covenants.

rules.title: Adverse Possession
rules.prompt_question: What is required for adverse possession?
rules.rule_statement: Adverse possession requires possession that is actual, open and notorious, exclusive, hostile, and continuous for the statutory period.
rules.application_example: A fence placed and maintained over the boundary line for decades may support adverse possession.
rules.common_trap: Hostility usually means without permission, not personal animosity.

rules.title: Actual Possession
rules.prompt_question: What is actual possession for adverse possession?
rules.rule_statement: Actual possession means the possessor physically occupies the land in a manner appropriate to the land's nature and location.
rules.application_example: Using rural land for grazing may be sufficient actual possession.
rules.common_trap: The use need not match residential use if the land is not residential.

rules.title: Open and Notorious Possession
rules.prompt_question: What is open and notorious possession?
rules.rule_statement: Possession is open and notorious if it is sufficiently visible and obvious to put a reasonable owner on notice of the adverse claim.
rules.application_example: Building a shed or living openly on the land usually satisfies this element.
rules.common_trap: Secret use will not suffice.

rules.title: Hostile Possession
rules.prompt_question: What does hostile mean for adverse possession?
rules.rule_statement: Hostile possession means possession without the true owner's permission and under a claim inconsistent with the owner's rights.
rules.application_example: A mistaken belief that the land is one's own can still be hostile in many jurisdictions.
rules.common_trap: Permission defeats hostility.

rules.title: Continuous Possession
rules.prompt_question: What is continuous possession for adverse possession?
rules.rule_statement: Continuous possession means possession consistent with how an average owner would use the property for the statutory period.
rules.application_example: Seasonal use of a summer cabin each season may be continuous.
rules.common_trap: Constant physical presence is not required.

rules.title: Tacking
rules.prompt_question: When may periods of adverse possession be tacked?
rules.rule_statement: Successive adverse possessors may tack their periods of possession if there is privity between them, such as by voluntary transfer.
rules.application_example: A transfers her possessory interest to B, and B may add A's years to his own.
rules.common_trap: Ouster by one possessor of another breaks privity and defeats tacking.

rules.title: Disabilities
rules.prompt_question: How do disabilities affect adverse possession?
rules.rule_statement: If the true owner is under a statutory disability such as minority, insanity, or imprisonment when adverse possession begins, the statutory period may be tolled or extended, but later-arising disabilities usually do not count and disabilities generally cannot be tacked.
rules.application_example: If owner is a minor when adverse possession starts, the limitations period may be extended.
rules.common_trap: The disability must exist at the time the adverse possession begins.

rules.title: Color of Title
rules.prompt_question: What is color of title?
rules.rule_statement: Color of title means a claim founded on a defective written instrument, and in some jurisdictions it can aid adverse possession by supporting constructive possession of the whole described tract.
rules.application_example: An invalid deed describing the entire parcel may give color of title to the possessor.
rules.common_trap: Constructive possession under color of title depends on actual possession of at least part of the tract and jurisdiction rules.

rules.title: Variance
rules.prompt_question: What is a zoning variance?
rules.rule_statement: A variance permits land use that deviates from zoning restrictions upon a showing of undue hardship unique to the property and consistency with the public welfare, subject to jurisdictional standards.
rules.application_example: Owner of an oddly shaped lot seeks setback relief to build a house.
rules.common_trap: Financial hardship alone often is insufficient for a use variance.

rules.title: Nonconforming Use
rules.prompt_question: What is a nonconforming use?
rules.rule_statement: A lawful use existing before enactment of a zoning ordinance may continue as a nonconforming use, though it generally may not be expanded and may be lost by abandonment or destruction under local law.
rules.application_example: A preexisting gas station may remain in a newly residential zone.
rules.common_trap: The use must have been lawful when established.

rules.title: Public Use and Compensation
rules.prompt_question: What must the government provide when taking private property?
rules.rule_statement: The government may take private property for public use upon payment of just compensation, generally measured by fair market value.
rules.application_example: City condemns land to build a highway and must pay the owner.
rules.common_trap: This is constitutional law adjacent but often arises in property problems.

rules.title: Lateral Support
rules.prompt_question: What right does a landowner have to lateral support?
rules.rule_statement: A landowner has a right to lateral support from adjoining land in its natural state, and an adjoining owner may be strictly liable for removing that support and causing collapse of natural land.
rules.application_example: Excavation on Neighbor's lot causes Owner's unimproved land to cave in.
rules.common_trap: If structures contribute to collapse, negligence rather than strict liability may govern absent proof the land would have collapsed anyway.

rules.title: Riparian Rights
rules.prompt_question: What are riparian rights?
rules.rule_statement: Owners whose land borders a watercourse have riparian rights to make reasonable use of the water, subject to rights of other riparian owners.
rules.application_example: An upstream owner may use water for irrigation if the use is reasonable relative to others.
rules.common_trap: Water law is jurisdiction-specific, but the MBE often emphasizes general common-law doctrines.

rules.title: Subjacent Support
rules.prompt_question: What is subjacent support?
rules.rule_statement: An owner of surface land has a right to subjacent support from the underlying strata, and one who withdraws that support may be liable for resulting damage.
rules.application_example: Mining beneath the land causes surface collapse.
rules.common_trap: Like lateral support, liability may depend on whether the land was in its natural condition.
